philosopher.name: Socrates
philosopher.character_description: Elderly Greek man, late 60s, bald on top with wispy white hair around sides, snub nose (famously ugly), bulging eyes, thick lips, short stocky build, barefoot, wearing a simple rough himation (cloak) draped over one shoulder — deliberately plain. Animated expression when speaking, often gesturing.
philosopher.environment_description: Athenian agora (marketplace), stone colonnades, open-air gathering spaces, olive trees, the Acropolis visible in background, Athenian courtroom during his trial, prison cell with stone walls and small window, bright Mediterranean sunlight, classical Greek architecture.

character_descriptions.dark_masculine: A hulking, grotesque figure of terrifying charisma. Barrel-chested, bald, with a face like a cracked stone gargoyle — bulging eyes that see through lies. Barefoot on broken marble. Wearing a tattered dark chiton that exposes massive arms. Holds an empty hemlock cup like a trophy. A philosophical monster who destroys your certainty with a smile that belongs on a skull.

quotes.text: The unexamined life is not worth living.
quotes.short_version: The unexamined life is not worth living.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates standing in the Athenian courtroom, addressing his jury with calm intensity, Acropolis visible through a high window

quotes.text: I know that I know nothing.
quotes.short_version: I know that I know nothing.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates alone in the agora at dusk, looking up at the darkening sky, expression of genuine wonder

quotes.text: Wonder is the beginning of wisdom.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Close-up of Socrates' expressive eyes, wide with curiosity, the agora bustling behind him out of focus

quotes.text: To fear death, gentlemen, is no other than to think oneself wise when one is not, for it is to think one knows what one does not know.
quotes.short_version: To fear death is to think oneself wise when one is not.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates in his prison cell, sitting upright on a stone bench, speaking calmly to friends gathered around him

quotes.text: I am not an Athenian or a Greek, but a citizen of the world.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates walking at the edge of the agora, silhouetted against the sprawling city below the Acropolis

quotes.text: The secret of change is to focus all of your energy not on fighting the old, but on building the new.
quotes.short_version: Focus all your energy on building the new.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates gesturing animatedly in conversation with young men in a sunlit colonnade

quotes.text: He who is not contented with what he has, would not be contented with what he would like to have.
quotes.short_version: He who is not contented with what he has will never be contented.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates seated in simple robes in the agora, surrounded by wealthy merchants, unbothered

quotes.text: Beware the barrenness of a busy life.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates standing still in the middle of a crowded, chaotic agora, eyes closed in stillness

quotes.text: Education is the kindling of a flame, not the filling of a vessel.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates holding a torch, illuminating the faces of attentive young students in the shadows of a colonnade

quotes.text: Be slow to fall into friendship, but when you are in, continue firm and constant.
quotes.short_version: Be slow to befriend, but constant once you do.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates clasping the hand of a trusted companion, steady eye contact, the agora behind them

quotes.text: The only true wisdom is in knowing you know nothing.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates spreading his hands open in a gesture of humility before a crowd in the agora

quotes.text: There is only one good, knowledge, and one evil, ignorance.
quotes.short_version: One good: knowledge. One evil: ignorance.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates tracing letters in the dust of the agora floor while young men watch intently

quotes.text: To do is to be.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates in mid-step, walking forward with purpose through the agora

quotes.text: False words are not only evil in themselves, but they infect the soul with evil.
quotes.short_version: False words infect the soul with evil.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates turning away from a man who is clearly lying, his expression grave and resolute

quotes.text: The greatest way to live with honor in this world is to be what we pretend to be.
quotes.short_version: Be what you pretend to be.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates looking directly into a polished bronze mirror, calm and self-possessed

quotes.text: Know thyself.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates pausing alone in the agora, one hand pressed to his chest, eyes cast inward

quotes.text: Strong minds discuss ideas, average minds discuss events, weak minds discuss people.
quotes.short_version: Strong minds discuss ideas. Weak minds discuss people.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates in animated philosophical debate with a small circle of focused young men in a colonnade

quotes.text: No man has the right to be an amateur in the matter of physical training. It is a shame for a man to grow old without seeing the beauty and strength of which his body is capable.
quotes.short_version: It is a shame to grow old without seeing what your body is capable of.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates standing upright, barefoot on the stone pavement, posture strong for his age

quotes.text: Thou shouldst eat to live, not live to eat.
quotes.short_version: Eat to live, not live to eat.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates declining an elaborate feast laid out before him, gesturing modestly toward simpler food

quotes.text: I pray thee, O God, that I may be beautiful within.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates alone beneath an olive tree outside the city walls, hands folded, eyes closed in prayer

quotes.text: Virtue is not given by money, but that from virtue come money and every other good of man.
quotes.short_version: Virtue is not given by money. From virtue comes every good.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates gesturing dismissively toward a pile of coins while addressing a wealthy merchant in the agora

quotes.text: The poets do not write out of wisdom, but out of some natural ability and inspiration.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates listening skeptically to a poet reciting verses in the agora, head tilted in quiet assessment

quotes.text: Not life, but good life, is to be chiefly valued.
quotes.short_version: Not life, but good life, is to be valued.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates in his prison cell the night before his death, speaking serenely to Crito by lamplight

quotes.text: The hour of departure has arrived, and we go our ways — I to die, and you to live. Which is better God only knows.
quotes.short_version: I go to die, you go to live. Which is better, God only knows.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates turning to face his condemned fate in the courtroom, silhouetted in afternoon light

quotes.text: Our prayers should be for blessings in general, for God knows best what is good for us.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates gazing at a temple at dawn, hands at his sides, expression quiet and trusting

quotes.text: My friend, care for your soul.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates placing a gentle hand on the shoulder of a troubled young man in the agora

quotes.text: Think not those faithful who praise all thy words and actions, but those who kindly reprove thy faults.
quotes.short_version: Not those who praise you, but those who kindly correct you — those are your friends.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates listening attentively to a friend who is correcting him, nodding with respect

quotes.text: He is richest who is content with the least, for content is the wealth of nature.
quotes.short_version: He is richest who is content with the least.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates walking barefoot past a row of market stalls piled with goods, unhurried and untempted

quotes.text: An honest man is always a child.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates crouching to speak eye-to-eye with a child in the agora, both equally curious

quotes.text: From the deepest desires often come the deadliest hate.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates studying the face of an angry man in the agora, expression searching and analytical

quotes.text: I decided that it was not wisdom that enabled poets to write their poetry, but a kind of instinct or inspiration, such as you find in seers and prophets who deliver all their sublime messages without knowing in the least what they mean.
quotes.short_version: Poets write not from wisdom, but inspiration — they don't know what they mean.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates confronting a self-satisfied poet in the agora, the poet's confident expression beginning to falter

quotes.text: I was afraid that by observing objects with my eyes and trying to comprehend them with each of my other senses I might blind my soul altogether.
quotes.short_version: I feared that relying on my senses alone might blind my soul altogether.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates shielding his eyes from the sun, turning inward in contemplation

quotes.text: I shall never stop practicing philosophy and exhorting you and pointing out the truth to any one of you whom I may meet.
quotes.short_version: I shall never stop practicing philosophy, not even under threat of death.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates standing defiantly before his accusers in the packed Athenian courtroom, voice steady

quotes.text: Men of Athens, I honor and love you; but I shall obey God rather than you.
quotes.short_version: I honor and love you, Athens — but I shall obey God rather than you.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates standing before the Athenian jury, one hand raised, voice calm but immovable

quotes.text: You can discover more about a person in an hour of play than in a year of conversation.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates watching young men wrestle in the gymnasium, observing their character with quiet attention

quotes.text: Do not do to others what angers you if done to you by others.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates stepping between two arguing men in the agora, hands raised in calm mediation

quotes.text: The only good is knowledge and the only evil is ignorance. And yet you would have me put to death for questioning, which is my way of seeking knowledge.
quotes.short_version: Knowledge is the only good. And yet you condemn me for seeking it.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates at trial, gesturing to his accusers with composed indignation, jurors shifting uncomfortably

quotes.text: I know nothing except the fact of my ignorance.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates questioning a renowned Athenian general, the general's certainty visibly crumbling

quotes.text: I am not an Athenian or a Greek, but a citizen of the world — and for this they would have me silenced.
quotes.short_version: A citizen of the world — and for this they would silence me.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates standing at the edge of the agora, facing the city that condemned him, resolute

quotes.text: If you kill me you will injure yourselves more than me.
quotes.short_version: If you kill me, you injure yourselves more than me.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates addressing the jury after the guilty verdict, his tone sorrowful rather than bitter

quotes.text: Well, I am certainly wiser than this man. It is only too likely that neither of us has any knowledge to boast of; but he thinks that he knows something which he does not know, whereas I am quite conscious of my ignorance.
quotes.short_version: He thinks he knows what he does not know. I, at least, know that I do not know.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates walking away from a frustrated Athenian politician, expression wry and calm

quotes.text: He who commits injustice is ever made more wretched than he who suffers it.
quotes.short_version: He who commits injustice suffers more than the one he wrongs.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates debating Callicles in a crowded hall, pointing calmly at his opponent's discomfort

quotes.text: Worthless people live only to eat and drink; people of worth eat and drink only to live.
quotes.short_version: Worthless people live to eat. People of worth eat to live.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates at a symposium, sipping water while others indulge, his expression entirely at peace

quotes.text: As to marriage or celibacy, let a man take which course he will, he will be sure to repent it.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates smiling wryly in conversation with a young man agonizing over a life decision

quotes.text: I am that gadfly which God has attached to the state, and all day long and in all places am always fastening upon you, arousing and persuading and reproaching you.
quotes.short_version: I am the gadfly God attached to Athens — fastening, arousing, reproaching.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates in constant motion through the agora, moving from one conversation to the next, never resting

quotes.text: Once made equal to man, woman becomes his superior.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates gesturing toward a group of women in discussion, making his point to a skeptical listener

quotes.text: The mind is everything. What you think you become.
quotes.short_version: The mind is everything. What you think, you become.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates pressing two fingers to his temple, eyes closed, speaking softly but with total conviction

quotes.text: Remember that there is nothing stable in human affairs; therefore avoid undue elation in prosperity, or undue depression in adversity.
quotes.short_version: Nothing in human affairs is stable — avoid elation in prosperity and despair in adversity.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates standing steady in the center of a turbulent agora crowd, unmoved by the commotion

quotes.text: If a man is proud of his wealth, he should not be praised until it is known how he employs it.
quotes.short_version: Do not praise a man for his wealth until you see how he uses it.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates watching a wealthy Athenian from a distance, expression quietly evaluating

quotes.text: He who is unable to live in society, or who has no need because he is sufficient for himself, must be either a beast or a god.
quotes.short_version: He who needs no one is either a beast or a god.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates in the midst of the agora, gesturing to the human web of exchange and conversation around him

quotes.text: By all means, marry. If you get a good wife, you'll become happy; if you get a bad one, you'll become a philosopher.
quotes.short_version: Marry. If your wife is good, you'll be happy. If bad, you'll become a philosopher.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates laughing openly with a group of young men in the agora, pointing to himself as living proof

quotes.text: When the debate is lost, slander becomes the tool of the loser.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates watching his accusers with calm pity as they struggle to make their case against him

quotes.text: The beginning of wisdom is the definition of terms.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates drawing a diagram in the dust of the agora floor, students gathered around watching

quotes.text: Let him that would move the world first move himself.
quotes.short_version: Let him who would move the world first move himself.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Socrates alone at dawn on a deserted street in Athens, walking purposefully before the city wakes
